segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an upbeat, electronic background music as the video displays a close-up of a man holding a camera, focusing on its lens against a blurred outdoor background, setting a photography theme. This transitions to a top-down view of a desk cluttered with photography and design tools, including a laptop, camera lenses, color swatches, and markers, accompanied by a text overlay stating 'The creative photo-editing software designed by photographers, for photographers.' The scene then shifts to a vibrant, neon-lit urban environment featuring a woman sitting on a textured floor, with text highlighting '8 software' and listing tools like Nik Color Efex, Nik Silver Efex, and Nik Viveza. As the background music continues subtly, a male voice begins speaking, stating, "We're going to be discussing precise editing made simple, using Nik Viveza." The visual transitions to a webinar slide titled 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos,' presented by Dan Hughes, with a circular editing interface overlay on the woman’s image. The speaker elaborates, "This is the stuff we're going to talk about basically today... in a nutshell, we're talking about controlling light and color using Viveza." He continues, explaining that editing can be done "globally... adjusting the entire image all at once, and we can do it selectively... on particular objects or in particular areas, based upon where we place control points." The visuals show a static slide with orange and black text detailing Nik Viveza’s capabilities—precise color manipulation using Control Points, new presets, and a redesigned interface—emphasizing ease and control in editing outdoor photos. The speaker concludes, "If you're brand new to the Nik Collection, no worries, we're going to be discussing what a control point is..." The hexagonal orange and black Nik Collection logo consistently appears in the bottom right corner throughout the latter frames.

A male speaker explains the goal of creating depth in the image using color contrast, detailing how 'control points' will be used to enhance green areas, making them darker and more contrasted, and how warmer-toned flowers will be warmed up further to create separation from the cooler greens. For the first 35 seconds, the interface remains static, with all 'Global Adjustments' and 'Selective Tones' sliders set to 0%, indicating no edits have been applied. The status bar at the bottom confirms the file being edited is '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, captured with a NIKON Z 7 at ISO 800. As the speaker transitions to discussing the right-hand side of the interface, at 0:36, the user interacts by clicking the 'Histogram' option, which expands to reveal a color histogram with RGB and L (luminance) channels. segments.integrated_caption: The video segment showcases a user interacting with the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, focused on adjusting the color of green foliage in a close-up image. A male speaker provides a running commentary, initially discussing color theory and the "color wheel," then introducing a "cheat sheet" that "Red and Cyan work against each other." Visually, the user begins by adjusting the 'Blue' slider from 0% to -100%, which visibly shifts the foliage toward a more yellowish-green hue. The speaker explains that "if I click on the red slider and I start adding red, what I'm doing is I'm removing Cyan," and that sliding it to the left adds Cyan, causing a "shift and change" as the user reduces the 'Red' slider from 0% to -100%, further altering the color balance. The speaker briefly notes an interface quirk where double-clicking a slider doesn't return it to its default. Subsequently, the 'Green' slider is adjusted from 0% to -100%, causing the leaves to take on a more intense, almost neon green appearance. The speaker clarifies that adjusting green either adds green (sliding right) or removes green (sliding left), which "would be adding magenta." Throughout these adjustments, the image remains centered in the workspace with the control point highlighted over the foliage. The bottom status bar consistently displays the file name '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' and camera metadata (NIKON Z 7, ISO 800), while the 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons and the Nik Collection logo remain visible, indicating non-destructive adjustments.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment features a user editing a landscape photograph within the Viveza 3 interface, part of the Nik Collection. The image displays a serene scene with a lake, a green hillside, trees, and a farm with a red barn and white house under a cloudy sky. A male voice is heard stating, "control point into the sky, and let's just let's just boom out the structure." The user places a control point on the sky area and adjusts the 'Structure' slider in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel, increasing it from 0% to 92%. The speaker explains, "the structure tool here is going to bring that texture... bring out the contrast in the texture that is in the cloud, we're just accentuating it." He acknowledges that "rarely are you going to add control points and add 90% structure... for the sake of our demonstration, it does add quite a lot of impact." Following this, the speaker announces, "we're going to add a little bit of saturation," and the user increases the 'Saturation' slider to 64% to make the colors more vivid. The speaker then states, "I think I'm going to actually add a little bit of red," as the user adjusts the 'Red' slider from 0% to 25% to add warmth and richness, likely affecting the barn. The speaker clarifies that "what adding red is going to do is it's going to remove cyan, and it'll actually make those clouds look a little bit more... kind of neutral." Throughout the segment, the Nik Collection logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and the status bar indicates the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, captured with a Nikon D850 at ISO 100. The 'Apply' and 'Cancel' buttons remain visible, but the user does not apply the changes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a user actively editing a landscape photograph within the Viveza 3 interface, part of the Nik Collection. A male voice narrates, highlighting the utility of control points as a "really nice feature" built into Viveza and other Nik plugins for grouping adjustments. The image, featuring a lake, green hillside, trees, and a farm with a red barn and white house under a cloudy sky, is being refined. The user first adjusts the 'Structure' slider in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel for 'Group 1' to 79%. Following this, the narrator expresses a desire to create "a little bit of layering" in the trees, aiming to "darken down some of the shadow areas just a touch" and "make them a little bit cooler." The user then adds 'Control Point 4' over the trees on the left, adjusting its 'Brightness' to -16%, 'Structure' to 30%, 'Warmth' to -31%, and 'Blue' to 0%. The narrator explains that instead of using red, green, and blue sliders, he'll "just move to the warmth slider and I'll remove some warmth," noting it "does something similar" and is "easy and fast" though offering "a little bit less control" than RGB. He then points out "deciduous trees here that are behind this line here right on the kind of coastline or the beach." Subsequently, 'Control Point 5' is placed over the farm area, with its 'Contrast' increased to 17% and 'Saturation' to 66%. Throughout this selective editing process, the 'Apply' and 'Cancel' buttons remain visible, the status bar displays 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab with NIKON D850 and ISO 100 details, and the Nik Collection logo is consistently present in the bottom right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male speaker explaining his intent to "cool down and darken down" one area of the landscape image in Viveza 3, while simultaneously "warming up and brightening up" the foreground and deciduous trees to create a "nice kind of contrast." The speaker then demonstrates the "duplicate control point trick" using "option click and drag" to efficiently add more adjustment points, which are seen being placed near the farm and distant mountains. As the user adjusts sliders for Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, Structure, Shadow, and Warmth in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel—for instance, increasing Contrast to 17% and Structure to 16% for one point, and reducing Brightness to -16% while increasing Saturation to 30% for another—the speaker elaborates that these actions are "accentuating the photographic layering" to achieve "more depth within the photograph." The interface consistently displays the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' and NIKON D850 camera metadata. Towards the end, the speaker highlights a "great feature and capability" of Viveza 3: its "ability to completely change the color" of elements within the image, as the user collapses the 'Control Points' panel, leaving the circular adjustment overlays visible. The Nik Collection logo remains in the bottom right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male voice explaining how control points in Viveza 3 are designed to make "really clean selections" on specific "object or an area." Visually, the user is actively working within the Viveza 3 photo editing software, displaying a landscape image of a lake, green hills, and a farm. Multiple control points are being placed across the sky and landscape. The speaker references a "red barn" as an example where a control point can achieve a "really nice clean selection," describing the process of placing a control point and potentially needing to "size the area of influence." He then evaluates the current selection, noting it's "pretty good" but might be "selecting some of the green in the foreground" which he might not want. This aligns with the visual action where the user selects 'Control Point 10' and precisely adjusts its strength from 20% to 14%, aiming to refine its placement and influence. To achieve "a more precise selection," the speaker introduces the "color selectivity sliders." Concurrently, the user toggles the 'Color Picker' tool, immediately switching the image to a black-and-white luminance preview, and begins adjusting the 'Luminance' and 'Chrominance' sliders under 'Color Selectivity' to refine the selection. The speaker emphasizes that these "brand new" sliders in the "newest version of Viveza" allow users to "hone in on the selection." The image remains in monochrome, allowing the user to clearly visualize the effect of these adjustments. The interface consistently displays the file name 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, camera details (NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON D850), and the Nik Collection logo. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens within the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, displaying a dark, low-light image of a farm with a barn and silo. A male speaker immediately begins explaining the tools, clarifying that "luminance would be brightness" and "chrominance would be the color values." The user is actively adjusting 'Control Point 10' in the 'Selective Adjustments' panel, with the image in a black-and-white luminance preview. As the speaker explains that sliding the 'Luminance' slider to the right "hones in more," making the selection smaller, the user first increases the 'Luminance' slider under 'Color Selectivity' from 50% to 100%, visibly brightening the selected farm area. The speaker then instructs, "Let me actually double click here. I'm going to zoom in, um, one to two," and the user follows by adjusting the zoom level from 1:1 to 1:2, revealing more detail. The speaker continues, explaining that sliding the 'Luminance' slider to the left "broadens out the selection," making the tolerance wider. The user then reduces the 'Luminance' slider back down to 0%, causing the image to darken again, before increasing it to 65% to achieve a balanced exposure for the farm. A slight adjustment is also made to the 'Chrominance' slider. Throughout these precise and deliberate actions, the bottom status bar confirms the file being edited is 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (NIKON D850, ISO 100), and the Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner. The speaker concludes by emphasizing the use of a combination of these sliders depending on the selection.

The image is displayed in a black-and-white luminance preview, indicating the use of the 'Color Picker' tool. The user first increases the 'Luminance' slider under 'Color Selectivity' from 50% to 100%, which brightens the selected area around the farm, making it more visible. They then adjust the zoom level from 1:1 to 1:2, revealing more detail in the image. The user subsequently reduces the 'Luminance' slider back down to 0%, causing the image to darken again, and then increases it to 65%, achieving a balanced exposure for the farm. The 'Chrominance' slider is also adjusted slightly, but the primary focus remains on luminance. The bottom status bar confirms the file being edited is 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (NIKON D850, ISO 100). The Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout. The user's actions are precise and deliberate, aiming to selectively brighten the farm structure without affecting the surrounding dark areas.

As he speaks, the user adjusts the 'Luminance' slider under 'Color Selectivity' for 'Control Point 10' from 38% to 65%, while 'Chrominance' remains at 65%. The speaker elaborates on options, mentioning using an eyedropper to "change that barn to that color" or clicking "this little color swatch that's to the right of the color picker label," which is what the user visually does. He adds, "I can choose whatever color I want." The user selects a color from the wheel, and the image's color balance shifts slightly, with the sky becoming more saturated. The speaker then discusses how, depending on the operating system, "your color picker's going to look like this or this or this or this because these are all the different options for the Mac based color picker," adding, "If you're on a PC, it's going to look different." segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a user interacting with the Viveza 3 photo editing software, displaying an outdoor landscape image featuring water, green trees, and a farm with a silo. A male narrator explains the tool's flexibility, allowing users to "hunt and peck" for colors. He notes the unusual choice of a blue barn, stating, "this barn was red, we're going to make it blue, and we're just going to see what happens." Visually, the user opens the 'Colors' dialog box, which presents a circular color wheel and a horizontal slider. The cursor moves to select a specific blue hue, fine-tuning it with the slider before clicking 'OK'. The narrator comments on the fun of making adjustments, even extreme ones, though he typically uses the technique for "slightly shift[ing] a color." He then narrates, "So we choose the color, we click okay," as the user applies the blue color adjustment to a control point on the farm area, indicated by a circular overlay. The 'Control Points' panel is active, with 'Control Point 10' selected. The user proceeds to adjust the 'Brightness' slider under 'Basic Adjustments' from 0% to -46%, darkening the selected area, and sets 'Luminance' to 38% and 'Chrominance' to 65% under 'Color Selectivity'. The image transitions from a neutral tone to a more vivid, selectively adjusted version, emphasizing the farm with enhanced color and contrast, also shifting from a black-and-white luminance preview to a vibrant outdoor scene with a red barn. The narrator then describes checking the work, stating, "I'm going to click outside of that control point. It's doing a pretty good job. I can actually see a couple small areas that are red." The zoom level changes from 1:1 to 1:2, and the user expands the 'Levels & Curves' panel. Throughout, the Nik Collection logo is visible in the bottom right, and the status bar shows the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, captured with a Nikon D850 at ISO 100, 24.0 MP, with 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons also visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male voice explaining the strategic placement of control points within the Viveza 3 interface, suggesting duplicating a control point to apply adjustments to "other things that we want blue." Visually, the user is engaged in a detailed photo editing session, manipulating control points on a landscape image of a farm by a body of water. The speaker describes using an "Option click and drag" to position a control point, noting he might be "still missing it" and suggesting a zoom. The interface, displaying the 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' file from DxO PhotoLab, initially shows the image at 1:1 zoom with multiple control points. The user then selects 'Control Point 11' and adjusts its 'Brightness' to -65%, followed by creating 'Control Point 12' near a white house, also setting its brightness to -65%. As the speaker states, "I'll zoom in one to two," the view transitions to a 1:2 zoom, revealing finer details of the farm, including a blue barn, silo, and the white house. The speaker continues to explain the need to "hone those luminance values and chrominance values," emphasizing looking at "the object itself, the barn itself," rather than just a selection, to "get the exact area selected and adjusted." The user, in parallel, refines adjustments, selecting 'Control Point 10' and modifying its 'Luminance' from 38% to 65% and 'Chrominance' from 63% to 45%. Two new control points, 'Control Point 13' and 'Control Point 14', are added near and on the roof of the white house, both with brightness set to -65%. The speaker concludes by highlighting how these precise, localized adjustments can "dramatically change the color of a barn" or "any object," with the Nik Collection logo consistently visible in the bottom right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a static view of Adobe Photoshop 2021, displaying an image of a woman on rocky terrain by the water at twilight, zoomed to 50%. The Layers panel shows an empty 'Layer 1' and the 'Background' image, while the Navigator and History panels are also visible. A male speaker confirms they are "over here in Photoshop" after switching host software. At 0:08, a black studio-style lamp with a glowing bulb appears in the top-right of the image canvas, which the speaker identifies as "the light source that's actually illuminating the model here." He explains that this photograph, featuring a serene coastal scene with distant boats under a dusky blue sky, was shot by his friend and colleague David Turner for a Maine media workshop. The speaker details the portrait lighting technique David used, involving setting the camera's white balance to an incandescent light source and gelling a flash "like this" with a CTO (color temperature orange) piece of plastic to create separation between the subject and background. The floating lamp, casting no visible light or shadow, serves as a visual representation of this technique rather than an active edit. The Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right, and the speaker concludes by stating their next step will be to use Viveza, a Nik Collection plugin, to refine the colors.

The speaker further illustrates how adding another control point can 'neutralize' an area, and later introduces the term 'constrictive control points,' before briefly mentioning the potential for a 'rabbit hole' when discussing 'color effects pro.' segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on an Adobe Photoshop 2021 editing session within the Viveza 3 plugin, displaying a sunlit photograph of a historic stone bridge with buildings and a small boat. A male voice explains the functionality of control points, noting how they allow for applying individual filters, acting as both "plus" and "minus" points for "color effects," either "putting the filter in or taking it out." He emphasizes that in Viveza, these "constricting control points" are used to "hone in the selections" and are not just negative, but "able to make other adjustments." As he speaks about removing and adding saturation to create "interesting layering," the user methodically adds control points to the yellow building facade. Initially, three are visible, and the user adds a fourth, then a fifth, and finally a sixth, each targeting different sections. The speaker continues, describing how one might "shrink that control point" and "place one over here," leading to a "checkerboarding effect" where some areas are "highly saturated" and others are not, demonstrating how "they are working with each other." Visually, the user adjusts the 'Saturation' slider for Control Points 4, 5, and 6 to 100%, visibly intensifying the yellow tones and enhancing the vibrancy of the targeted building sections. The 'Selective Adjustments' panel with 'Control Points' expanded is visible, and the Nik Collection logo remains in the bottom right corner throughout this precise, localized color enhancement process.

The user is working on a sunlit photograph of a historic stone bridge with buildings on either side and a small boat on the water below. The focus is on selective color adjustments using Control Points placed on the yellow building facade. Initially, three control points are visible, and the user adds a fourth, then a fifth, and finally a sixth, each targeting different sections of the building. The user adjusts the 'Saturation' slider for Control Point 4, increasing it to 100%, which visibly intensifies the yellow tones in that area. Subsequently, the user adds Control Point 5 and then Control Point 6, adjusting the saturation for each to 100% as well, further enhancing the vibrancy of the targeted building sections. The interface displays the 'Selective Adjustments' panel with 'Control Points' expanded, showing the current settings for each point. The Nik Collection logo remains consistently visible in the bottom right corner. The user does not interact with the 'Apply' or 'Cancel' buttons during this segment, and no warning dialog or other pop-ups appear. The overall atmosphere is one of precise, localized color enhancement, with the user methodically increasing saturation to achieve a more vivid and warm appearance in the building’s facade.

Visually, the user adjusts 'Brightness' to -33% and 'Saturation' to -39% for Control Point 8, then applies these changes before adding Control Point 9 over the boat and water. The speaker concludes by wishing viewers a "lovely and wonderful weekend," encouraging them to "enjoy your software, enjoy photography," and inviting them to the "next webinar." As he finishes, a cheerful, upbeat background music track begins.
